text: Unity tip: [SerializeField] private beats a public field almost every time. You keep encapsulation, but the Inspector still sees it. Fewer weird bugs from someone dragging the wrong object in.
Unity tip: caching GetComponent<T>() in Awake() instead of calling it inside Update() sounds trivial, but on a scene with hundreds of active objects it's a real, measurable frame-time difference.
Unity tip: ScriptableObjects make surprisingly good shared data containers, no scene reference hunting, no singleton boilerplate, and designers can tweak values without touching code.
Unity tip: object pooling isn't just for bullets and particles. Anything you Instantiate/Destroy in a loop, UI elements, enemies, pickups, is a GC spike waiting to happen. Pool it instead.
Unity tip: LINQ reads beautifully and allocates constantly. Fine in Start(), risky in Update(). If it runs every frame, a plain for-loop is usually worth the lost elegance.

Unity tip: coroutines are tied to a MonoBehaviour's lifecycle and get silently killed when the object is disabled. async/await doesn't have that safety net built in, you have to manage cancellation yourself or it keeps running against a destroyed object.

Unity tip: the Job System plus Burst compiler can turn a hot C# loop into near-native performance, but only if the code is burst-compatible, no managed references, no exceptions. Worth it for the right bottleneck, overkill for most code.

Unity tip: Resources.Load bundles everything into the build and never unloads automatically. Addressables give you real control over what's loaded, when, and when it's released, worth the setup cost once a project outgrows a handful of assets.

Unity tip: animation events firing a callback at a specific frame beat polling Animator.GetCurrentAnimatorStateInfo() every Update() to guess when a clip finished. Less code, no timing drift, no magic numbers.

Unity tip: physics runs on its own fixed timestep, separate from your frame rate. Reading Rigidbody state inside Update() instead of FixedUpdate() is a common source of jittery movement that's surprisingly hard to spot by eye.

Unity tip: [HideInInspector] on a public field keeps it serialised, so it still saves properly and shows up in Debug mode, but hides it from the normal Inspector view. Handy for fields other scripts should touch but nobody should edit by hand.

Unity tip: a static method marked [RuntimeInitializeOnLoadMethod] runs automatically at startup, no GameObject, no MonoBehaviour, nothing to drag into any scene at all. Setup code that used to need an empty placeholder object doesn't need one.

Unity tip: renaming an asset in the Project window feels harmless until saved data references it by name. A custom rename tool that records an alias map is one reliable way to keep old saves loading after a rename.

Unity tip: loading scenes by string name instead of build index means reordering your scene list in Build Settings can't silently break anything. Index-based loading breaks the moment someone reorders the list; string-based just doesn't care.

Unity tip: resetting a scene doesn't have to mean reloading it. Caching each object's original transform and restoring it on demand is a much faster iteration loop than a full scene reload every single test run.

C# tip: still reaching for List<T> out of habit? If you're calling Contains() a lot, a HashSet<T> turns that O(n) scan into an O(1) lookup. Same logic applies to Dictionary<TKey,TValue> when you're searching by key instead of by value.

C# tip: ToUpper() and ToLower() use the current culture by default. Under a Turkish locale, i.ToUpper() becomes İ, not I. Use ToUpperInvariant()/ToLowerInvariant() for anything comparison-related, like keys or codes.

C# tip: switch expressions (the => syntax) aren't just switch statements with less typing. Pattern matching on type and shape often replaces a whole chain of is/else-if checks with one readable block.

C# tip: two identical string literals are actually the same object in memory, interned automatically. Build that same string at runtime instead and it's a different object entirely. ReferenceEquals will surprise you if you don't know which case you're in.

C# tip: calling a member on a readonly field of a non-readonly struct forces a hidden defensive copy every time, even for a simple getter, because the compiler can't prove it won't mutate. Marking the struct itself readonly removes the copy entirely.

C# tip: a struct that implements an interface gets boxed, heap-allocated, the moment you store it in a variable typed as that interface instead of the concrete struct type. The value-type performance win quietly disappears at exactly that point.

C# tip: extension methods don't behave polymorphically. Which overload gets called is decided by the variable's compile-time type, not the object's actual runtime type, the opposite of how a virtual method call works.
XR dev tip: comfort beats fidelity. A locomotion system that's 90% as pretty but doesn't trigger motion sickness will outperform a flashier one every time. Test with people who aren't used to VR yet.
XR dev tip: frame rate matters more in VR than anywhere else. A dropped frame on a flat screen is a stutter; a dropped frame in a headset is nausea. Steady beats pretty, every time.
XR dev tip: always ship a seated and standing calibration path. Assuming everyone will stand and walk around locks out a chunk of your actual audience before they even start.

XR dev tip: foveated rendering renders the centre of your gaze at full resolution and the periphery lower, exploiting how human vision actually works. Free performance headroom, if your headset's eye tracking supports it.

XR dev tip: Meta's Platform SDK needs a callback pump alive at all times or every async call silently hangs forever, no error, no timeout, just nothing. One missing component and entitlement, identity, and colocation calls all just stop responding.

XR dev tip: don't force interactions to happen at a fixed height. What's comfortable eye-level for one player floats near someone else's chin for another. Always calibrate to the player, never assume a default height.

XR dev tip: dragging a UI element in VR isn't that different from dragging one on a screen once you project the controller's pointer onto the canvas in 3D space. Same drag-and-drop logic, just aimed differently.

XR dev tip: letting someone grab and reposition their entire workspace means temporarily disabling every CharacterController and flipping non-kinematic Rigidbodies to kinematic, then restoring the exact original state on release.

XR dev tip: in a colocated multiplayer session, only the host needs to scan the room. Everyone else just discovers the existing scan through the platform's APIs, no rescanning needed every time someone new joins.

XR dev tip: running Photon Fusion in Server mode instead of Host mode means a dedicated server is always the host. A player's headset can disconnect and reconnect without the whole session shutting down.

Web dev tip: bcrypt's cost factor isn't just a config number, it directly controls how long hashing takes. Set it too low and password hashes are crackable faster than they should be; too high and login itself gets noticeably slow.
Web dev tip: a JWT isn't encrypted by default, just signed. Anyone can decode and read the payload; the signature only proves it wasn't tampered with. Never put anything actually secret inside one.

Web dev tip: free-tier hosting cold starts are a real UX problem, not a rare edge case. A backend that's been idle for 15 minutes can take 20-30 seconds to wake up on the very request a real user is waiting on.

Web dev tip: a static site generator like Eleventy isn't magic, it's just running your templates once at build time instead of on every request. Not that different a mental model from baking a Unity build instead of running the editor live.

Web dev tip: localStorage persists across browser restarts and has no expiry by default, unlike a typical session. Storing a login token there means you're also on the hook for deciding when it actually goes stale.

Web dev tip: most transactional email APIs return success as soon as they've accepted your request for delivery, not once the email has actually landed. A 200 response only proves it entered the send queue; bounces show up later, usually via webhook.

Reminder for anyone building solo: shipped and imperfect beats perfect and unreleased. The projects I've learned the most from were the ones actually live, taking real traffic, breaking in ways a tutorial never would.
The best way to learn a new stack isn't a course, it's a real project with a real deadline and a real user who'll notice if it breaks. Discomfort is doing its job.

Nobody starts a solo project already knowing the stack. You start, you break sync across every device in the build, you fix it, and eventually you somehow understand Fusion networking. That's the whole method.
Writing the code is maybe half the job. Deploying it, watching it in production, and being the one who finds out when it breaks teaches you things a pull request review never will.
Every overnight side project I've seen had months of unglamorous commits nobody looked at first. The boring middle is the actual work, the launch is just the part people notice.
Deleting code you wrote three months ago because you know better now isn't a failure, it's the whole point of getting better. Attachment to old code is the real technical debt.
Writing the README before the feature is finished forces you to explain your own decisions to yourself, a great way to notice a design that doesn't actually make sense yet.
A side project that teaches you something is a win even if nobody else ever uses it. Traffic is nice. The learning was already banked the moment you shipped it.

A weekend of frantic coding followed by three weeks of nothing teaches less than a steady hour a day. Consistency compounds in a way bursts of motivation never do, and it's a lot harder to sustain than it sounds.
Comparing your unfinished project to someone else's finished one is a rigged comparison every single time. You're not seeing their first draft.

The best debugging sessions happen when you're chasing curiosity, not just a deadline. Understanding why something actually broke sticks with you in a way patching the symptom and moving on never does.
Asking a stupid question in public once beats silently struggling with it for a week. Most people in the room have the same question and are also too embarrassed to ask.
A tight deadline and a limited stack force decisions a blank slate never will. Constraints aren't the enemy of a good solution, more often they're exactly what produces one.
The point of building in public isn't the applause, it's that having an actual audience, even a small one, changes how carefully you think about what you ship.
